common_name: Jimmy Nardello Pepper (Sweet Italian Frying Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Jimmy Nardello is a Slow Food Ark of Taste heirloom — an irreplaceable variety of historical and culinary significance that merits preservation in home garden seed collections.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Jimmy Nardello is one of the most beloved heirloom sweet peppers in American home gardening — an Italian variety brought to Connecticut from Basilicata, Italy, by Giuseppa and Jimmy Nardello's family in 1887 and preserved in that family for nearly a century before being donated to the Seed Savers Exchange. The long, thin, curved, brilliant red fruits (8–10 inches, very thin walls) are among the sweetest of all peppers when fully ripe red — intensely concentrated sweet pepper flavor without any heat whatsoever. Jimmy Nardello is the premier sweet frying pepper: the thin walls mean the fruits cook in seconds in hot oil (traditional Italian fritto method) or over high heat on a grill, caramelizing immediately to a concentrated, smoky-sweet, almost jammy flavor that is entirely different from the fresh fruit character. The dried fruits are also exceptional for crushing into sweet paprika with excellent color and flavor. As an open-pollinated heirloom, seeds can be saved indefinitely and the variety preserved — Jimmy Nardello is recognized on the Slow Food Ark of Taste as a culturally significant food variety worth preserving. In zone 9, the plants are very productive from late spring through fall. The abundant, long, curved red fruits hanging from productive plants are visually beautiful and practically abundant.
sources: Slow Food USA Ark of Taste

common_name: Pimiento Pepper (Classic Sweet Thick-Walled Pimento Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Pimiento is the traditional variety for making pimiento cheese — the classic Southern spread requiring the specific thick-walled, sweet, very mild flesh of the true pimiento variety.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Pimiento (also spelled pimento) is the traditional thick-walled sweet pepper of Spanish and Southern American tradition — producing heart-shaped to rounded, medium-sized (3–4 inches) fruits with exceptionally thick, sweet, juicy walls and very mild, sweet flavor with no heat. The thick walls and very sweet, low-acid flesh are the defining qualities that distinguish pimiento from other sweet peppers: the wall thickness (often twice that of standard bell peppers) makes pimiento ideal for roasting, stuffing, and jar-packing, while the sweetness and mildness make it the original pepper for stuffed olives and the authentic ingredient in Southern pimiento cheese. The fruits ripen from green to a deep, vivid red and are at their best flavor when fully red. Pimiento is less commonly found in grocery stores than bell peppers (most commercial pimiento production goes to food processing), making it a particularly worthwhile home garden variety for cooks who want the authentic ingredient. The plants are productive, compact, and well-adapted to Gulf South growing conditions. In zone 9, pimiento peppers have two productive seasons: spring planting (February–March transplant, harvest May–July) and fall planting (August transplant, harvest October–November). The rich red color and exceptional thick-walled quality make pimiento one of the finest peppers for roasting whole.

common_name: Big Bertha Bell Pepper (Giant Extra-Large Sweet Bell Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Big Bertha's exceptional fruit size benefits from consistent fertility — apply balanced fertilizer at transplanting and again when fruits begin to size up for maximum development.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Big Bertha is one of the largest bell pepper varieties in general cultivation — producing extra-large, four-lobed fruits 7 inches long and 3.5–4 inches wide with very thick, crunchy walls and excellent sweet flavor. The enormous fruit size makes Big Bertha outstanding for stuffed pepper preparations where the cavity size determines how much filling can be used, and for slicing use where individual slices are large, substantial, and visually impressive. Big Bertha is a hybrid variety with disease resistance that includes tolerance to tobacco mosaic virus — an important practical quality in zone 9 gardens where virus pressure is a consistent challenge for sweet peppers. Fruits ripen from green to red, with the sweet flavor deepening and vitamin C content increasing significantly as the pepper fully ripens red. However, the thick-walled green stage is also excellent for fresh use. Big Bertha plants are vigorous and produce prolifically through the long Gulf South pepper growing season. The combination of extra-large fruit, good disease tolerance, and excellent fresh-eating quality makes Big Bertha one of the most popular large-fruited bell pepper varieties in American home gardening. Support plants with a stake or cage as the heavy fruits can cause branches to break.

common_name: Tanenashi Persimmon (Large Seedless Astringent Japanese Persimmon)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile to average soil in full sun; Tanenashi is self-fertile and does not require a pollinator — its consistent seedlessness (which requires no pollination) is one of its primary commercial and home garden advantages.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Tanenashi is the most widely planted Japanese persimmon cultivar in the American South — an astringent persimmon variety that produces large (3–4 inch diameter), round to slightly conical, bright orange-red fruits without seeds when grown without pollination. The seedless quality is one of Tanenashi's most important practical attributes — all commercial packing persimmons are seedless, and home-garden fresh eating quality improves without seeds in the soft, jellylike flesh. As an astringent persimmon, Tanenashi must be fully ripe (soft, completely yielding to pressure, like a water balloon) before eating or the tannins create an unpleasant mouth-puckering sensation; picking at the orange-red but still-firm stage and allowing to ripen at room temperature completes the conversion. The rich, sweet, pumpkin-like flavor of a fully ripe Tanenashi has no equivalent in temperate fruits. In zone 9, Tanenashi ripens in October through November and is one of the finest fall orchard fruits for the Gulf South — virtually pest-free, drought-tolerant once established, and producing heavily within 3–5 years of planting. The tree itself is ornamental: attractive dark-green summer foliage, spectacular orange-red fruit display in fall, and good fall foliage color before leaf drop.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Chocolate is a PVNA persimmon — it becomes non-astringent and develops brown flesh when pollinated and seeded, but remains astringent without seeds if grown without a pollinator nearby.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Chocolate is a PVNA (Pollination Variant Non-Astringent) Japanese persimmon — a type that produces sweet, non-astringent, crunchy-when-firm fruit with distinctive brown ('chocolate') flesh when pollinated and seeds are present, but remains astringent if no seeds develop from lack of pollination. The presence of a pollinator tree (Gailey, Maru, or another male or seeded cultivar) within 50 feet allows Chocolate to develop its characteristic dark brown, sweet, crunchy flesh that can be eaten firm at the orange-yellow stage — unlike astringent types that must be soft. This firm-eating non-astringent quality (shared by Fuyu) makes Chocolate outstanding for fresh use, salads, and eating out of hand. The unusual brown flesh color — resulting from tannin-anthocyanin reactions stimulated by seeds — is distinctive and has no direct equivalent among common temperate fruits. With a pollinator nearby, Chocolate produces medium-large, flat to slightly rounded, orange fruits with brown flesh that are ready from late September through November in zone 9. Without a pollinator, fruits are astringent and must ripen soft (similar to Hachiya) before eating. For maximum enjoyment, plant Chocolate near another persimmon variety for cross-pollination.
sources: University of California Agriculture and Natural Resources
